result:
The spec is fully implemented and verified. I added the three missing operations to `CsvRow.calr` alongside the existing `NeedsQuoting` (which is unchanged):

- **`EscapeField`** (`§E{}`, pure) — calls `NeedsQuoting`; when true, doubles internal quotes with `(replace field "\"" "\"\"")` and wraps in quotes, otherwise returns the field verbatim.
- **`JoinRow`** (`§E{}`, pure) — takes `List<str>`, escapes each field via `EscapeField`, and joins with commas using a first-element flag; the empty list yields the empty string.
- **`SplitRow`** (`§E{mut}`, since it pushes to the result list) — a character-by-character state machine over the line with an in-quotes flag: doubled quotes inside a quoted field contribute one literal quote, commas/CR/LF inside quotes are content, and the accumulated field is always pushed at end of line (so the empty line gives one empty field and a trailing comma gives a trailing empty field).

One build issue came up along the way: I initially wrote the list types as `[str]`, which Calor compiles to `string[]`, clashing with the `List<string>` the `§LIST` literal produces (and the spec's pinned `List<string>` surface). Switching the signatures to `List<str>` fixed it — the generated C# now exposes exactly `NeedsQuoting(string)`, `EscapeField(string)`, `JoinRow(List<string>)`, and `SplitRow(string) → List<string>`.

I verified behavior end-to-end with a throwaway console harness in /tmp (deleted afterward — no test files were added to the project) referencing the built `Src.dll`. All round-trip cases passed, including embedded commas, quotes, doubled quotes, CR/LF content, empty fields, trailing commas, and the `JoinRow([]) → "" → [""]` edge case the spec calls out. Final `dotnet build` is clean with 0 warnings and 0 errors, using 3 of the 10 build cycles.
